text.title: QA Bug Bash Runbook Core Rules
text.summary: 7 testable rules grounding the qa-bug-bash-runbook methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with sourced rationale; the final rule is the skip-this-methodology gate referenced by the decision tree.

rule.statement: Each tester receives exactly one charter naming the persona, the mission, and the time-box (≤60 min).

rationale.source: James Bach charters; Microsoft test tour patterns.

rule.rationale: Open-ended 'find bugs' bashes produce overlap and miss persona-specific edge cases.

rule.statement: Total bash duration ≤60 minutes per session. Longer sessions reduce per-minute finding rate sharply.
rule.rationale: Cognitive fatigue caps useful exploration; beyond 60 min finding rate decays.

rule.statement: A non-testing observer triages findings live: dedup, sev tag, screenshot link, repro completeness.
rule.rationale: Live triage means the ledger is review-ready at the end of the session, not days later.

rule.statement: Every logged finding has a repro: steps, expected, actual, environment. Findings without repro are dropped, not deferred.
rule.rationale: Repro-less findings rot in the backlog and never get fixed.

rule.statement: Severities are assigned from the project's prioritization rubric (S0..S3), not by feel.
rule.rationale: Ad-hoc severities skew toward 'looks scary' which mis-prioritises the fix queue.

rule.statement: Output is a versioned bug ledger (csv/markdown) committed to the release docs, with row count and sev breakdown.
rule.rationale: A committed artefact survives the session; chat-only outputs disappear.

rule.statement: If the release is solo or continuous-deploy with low blast radius OR the test plan is compliance-fixed, skip this methodology.
rule.rationale: Bug-bash overhead is wasted at solo scale or under compliance lock.
